message.content:
You are an independent blind verifier (verifier B) for the hc-assay hardening campaign at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay (branch harden/pass-10). Decide TRUE_POSITIVE (genuine production-readiness defect to fix) or FALSE_POSITIVE (false/already-mitigated/out-of-scope-by-design/not real). Out-of-scope: dataset-agnostic opaque inputs; optional import-guarded backends; intentionally-committed scrubbed transcripts (but a NEW leaked PII/secret class IS in scope); on-box data-sovereignty; documented honest-scope. Read real code and REPRODUCE where testable (cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && source .venv/bin/activate && python ...; "python -m pytest"; gh). Judge ONLY P10-MO-1.

FINDING P10-MO-1 (high): Provenance to_records/from_records roundtrip silently fails verification for set/frozenset payload values
Location: src/assay_engine/provenance.py (entries_to_records / from_records / _frozen freeze/unfreeze + canonical_json)
Claim: A payload value that is a set/frozenset (hashable, accepted by freeze) does not roundtrip through to_records→from_records identically (JSON has no set type; canonical serialization and re-thaw differ), so verify_records on the rebuilt chain FAILS (hash mismatch) — or the set is silently reordered/converted, breaking the tamper-evidence guarantee for a legitimately-recorded payload. VERIFY: record a trail entry with a frozenset/set value, run entries_to_records→from_records→verify_records, and check whether it verifies or raises/mismatches.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll verify this finding by examining the provenance code and reproducing the claimed behavior.

content.content:
1	"""Append-only provenance trail (GOVERNANCE §3, W3C PROV-DM style).
2	
3	Every action a study takes is recorded, in order, before the next executes: ingestion, the
4	blind baseline build, each discovered/locked hypothesis, every gate decision, each verdict,
5	the source scorecard, the final report. The trail is the spine of reproducibility and of the
6	independence claim — "no step can be retroactively removed or reordered" (GOVERNANCE §3).
7	
8	The trail enforces that structurally, not by promise:
9	
10	- **Append-only.** Entries are added through :meth:`ProvenanceTrail.record` only; there is no
11	  remove/edit/reorder API and the exposed view is an immutable tuple of frozen entries.
12	- **Chained.** Each entry carries ``prev_hash`` and an ``entry_hash`` over
13	  ``(prev_hash, seq, kind, summary, payload, timestamp)`` using the engine's one type-faithful
14	  serializer (:mod:`assay_engine._canonical`), rooted at a fixed genesis.
15	
16	Integrity — honest scope, two tiers:
17	
18	- **Unkeyed (default).** The chain is plain SHA-256. It is *tamper-evident against naive
19	  tampering and accidental corruption*: editing one entry without recomputing downstream
20	  hashes, reordering, or deleting an entry is caught by :func:`verify_records`. It is **not**
21	  forgery-proof: a motivated party who controls the serialized bytes can edit a payload and
22	  recompute the whole genesis-rooted chain, and the result verifies. Unkeyed integrity buys
23	  "is this a valid chain?", not "is this *the* chain the engine produced."
24	- **Keyed (``secret=...``).** Pass an on-box secret (as pre-registration does, ADR-0009) and
25	  the chain is HMAC-SHA256. The head cannot be recomputed without the secret, so a downstream
26	  store cannot silently rewrite history and re-seal it; :func:`verify_records` must be given
27	  the same secret. This is local tamper-*resistance*; non-repudiable third-party attestation of
28	  the trail's time remains the same pluggable concern as pre-registration, out of scope here.
29	"""
30	
31	from __future__ import annotations
32	
33	import datetime as _dt
34	import hashlib
35	import hmac
36	import threading
37	from dataclasses import dataclass
38	from typing import Any, Callable, Mapping, cast
39	
40	from assay_engine._canonical import canonical_json
41	from assay_engine._frozen import freeze_mapping, unfreeze
42	
43	_UTC = _dt.timezone.utc
44	_GENESIS = "assay-provenance:v1:genesis"
45	
46	Clock = Callable[[], _dt.datetime]
47	
48	
49	class ProvenanceError(RuntimeError):
50	    """The provenance trail is inconsistent (a broken chain) or an entry cannot be recorded."""
51	
52	
53	@dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True)
54	class ProvenanceEntry:
55	    """One immutable, chained record of a single action."""
56	
57	    seq: int
58	    kind: str
59	    summary: str
60	    payload: Mapping[str, Any]
61	    timestamp: str  # ISO-8601 UTC
62	    prev_hash: str
63	    entry_hash: str
64	
65	    def __post_init__(self) -> None:
66	        object.__setattr__(self, "payload", freeze_mapping(self.payload))
67	
68	
69	def _digest(
70	    seq: int,
71	    kind: str,
72	    summary: str,
73	    payload: Mapping[str, Any],
74	    timestamp: str,
75	    prev_hash: str,
76	    secret: bytes | None,
77	) -> str:
78	    try:
79	        msg = canonical_json(
80	            ["assay-prov:v1", prev_hash, seq, kind, summary, dict(payload), timestamp]
81	        ).encode("utf-8")
82	    except (ValueError, TypeError, RecursionError) as exc:
83	        # RecursionError (deeply nested payload) is a RuntimeError, not Value/TypeError — catch it
84	        # too so the entrypoints surface a typed ProvenanceError, never a raw exception (#89).
85	        raise ProvenanceError(
86	            f"provenance payload for {kind!r} cannot be serialized reproducibly ({exc}); "
87	            "record only JSON-native / hashable values of bounded depth"
88	        ) from None
89	    if secret is None:
90	        return hashlib.sha256(msg).hexdigest()
91	    return hmac.new(secret, msg, "sha256").hexdigest()
92	
93	
94	def _utc_now() -> _dt.datetime:
95	    return _dt.datetime.now(tz=_UTC)
96	
97	
98	class ProvenanceTrail:
99	    """An append-only, chained audit trail for one study run.
100	
101	    ``secret`` (>= 16 bytes) keys the chain with HMAC so a serialized trail cannot be forged
102	    without it (see module docstring). ``clock`` injects the timestamp source (deterministic
103	    tests); it must return a timezone-aware ``datetime``.
104	    """
105	
106	    def __init__(self, *, secret: bytes | None = None, clock: Clock | None = None) -> None:
107	        if secret is not None and (not isinstance(secret, (bytes, bytearray)) or len(secret) < 16):
108	            raise ValueError("provenance secret must be >= 16 bytes of key material")
109	        self._entries: list[ProvenanceEntry] = []
110	        self._secret: bytes | None = bytes(secret) if secret is not None else None
111	        self._clock: Clock = clock or _utc_now
112	        # serialize the read-compute-append so concurrent record() calls cannot interleave and
113	        # corrupt the seq/prev_hash chain (#114)
114	        self._lock = threading.Lock()
115	
116	    def record(self, kind: str, summary: str, **payload: Any) -> ProvenanceEntry:
117	        """Append one entry. Returns it. The only way to add to the trail. Thread-safe."""
118	        if not kind:
119	            raise ProvenanceError("provenance entry kind must be non-empty")
120	        with self._lock:
121	            return self._record_locked(kind, summary, payload)
122	
123	    def _record_locked(
124	        self, kind: str, summary: str, payload: Mapping[str, Any]
125	    ) -> ProvenanceEntry:
126	        seq = len(self._entries)
127	        prev_hash = self._entries[-1].entry_hash if self._entries else _GENESIS
128	        try:
129	            instant = self._clock()
130	        except Exception as exc:  # noqa: BLE001 — the clock is an injected dependency
131	            raise ProvenanceError(f"provenance clock raised: {exc}") from None
132	        if not isinstance(instant, _dt.datetime):
133	            raise ProvenanceError("provenance clock must return a datetime")
134	        try:
135	            # utcoffset()/astimezone()/isoformat() can raise on a hostile datetime subclass or an
136	            # out-of-range value — keep the typed-error contract (#96).
137	            if instant.tzinfo is None or instant.utcoffset() is None:
138	                raise ProvenanceError("provenance clock must return a timezone-aware datetime")
139	            timestamp = instant.astimezone(_UTC).isoformat()
140	        except ProvenanceError:
141	            raise
142	        except Exception as exc:  # noqa: BLE001
143	            raise ProvenanceError(f"provenance timestamp could not be normalized: {exc}") from None
144	        entry_hash = _digest(seq, kind, summary, payload, timestamp, prev_hash, self._secret)
145	        try:
146	            entry = ProvenanceEntry(
147	                seq=seq,
148	                kind=kind,
149	                summary=summary,
150	                payload=payload,
151	                timestamp=timestamp,
152	                prev_hash=prev_hash,
153	                entry_hash=entry_hash,
154	            )
155	        except (ValueError, TypeError, RecursionError) as exc:
156	            raise ProvenanceError(
157	                f"provenance entry for {kind!r} could not be frozen ({exc})"
158	            ) from None
159	        self._entries.append(entry)
160	        return entry
161	
162	    def as_recorder(self) -> Callable[[Any], None]:
163	        """A :data:`gates.ProvenanceRecorder` bound to this trail (records GateDecisions).
164	
165	        Reads each attribute exactly once and defensively, so a booby-trapped decision object
166	        (a property that raises) surfaces a typed ``ProvenanceError`` rather than a raw error.
167	        """
168	
169	        def _record(decision: Any) -> None:
170	            try:
171	                gate = getattr(decision, "gate", None)
172	                approved = bool(getattr(decision, "approved", False))
173	                reason = getattr(decision, "reason", "")
174	                evidence = dict(getattr(decision, "evidence", {}) or {})
175	            except Exception as exc:  # noqa: BLE001 — the decision is on the trust boundary
176	                raise ProvenanceError(
177	                    f"could not read gate decision for provenance: {exc}"
178	                ) from None
179	            self.record(
180	                "gate",
181	                f"gate {gate!r}: {'approved' if approved else 'blocked'}",
182	                gate=gate,
183	                approved=approved,
184	                reason=reason,
185	                evidence=evidence,
186	            )
187	
188	        return _record
189	
190	    @property
191	    def entries(self) -> tuple[ProvenanceEntry, ...]:
192	        return tuple(self._entries)
193	
194	    @property
195	    def head(self) -> str:
196	        """The current chain head (last entry_hash, or the genesis if empty)."""
197	        return self._entries[-1].entry_hash if self._entries else _GENESIS
198	
199	    def __len__(self) -> int:
200	        return len(self._entries)
201	
202	    def verify(self) -> None:
203	        """Recompute the chain; raise :class:`ProvenanceError` on any inconsistency."""
204	        verify_records(self._entries, secret=self._secret)
205	
206	    def to_records(self) -> tuple[dict[str, Any], ...]:
207	        """Serialize to plain dicts (for a persistent store); re-checkable via :func:`verify_records`."""
208	        return entries_to_records(self._entries)
209	
210	
211	def entries_to_records(
212	    entries: "list[ProvenanceEntry] | tuple[ProvenanceEntry, ...]",
213	) -> tuple[dict[str, Any], ...]:
214	    """Serialize loose entries (e.g. ``StudyResult.provenance``) to plain dicts (#F-045).
215	
216	    The same shape :meth:`ProvenanceTrail.to_records` produces, so a caller holding only the
217	    returned entries — not the trail object — can still persist a re-checkable trail.
218	
219	    Keying is NOT embedded (pass 5, #H-017): a keyed (HMAC) trail and an unkeyed (SHA-256) trail
220	    serialize identically — the records carry no key material and no trusted algorithm marker (one
221	    would be spoofable since it is not itself part of the per-entry digest). The verifier must
222	    therefore know out-of-band whether the trail was keyed and supply the same ``secret`` to
223	    :func:`from_records` / :func:`verify_records`; that is deliberate key management, not a gap —
224	    ``verify_records`` fails closed under the wrong/absent secret.
225	    """
226	    return tuple(
227	        {
228	            "seq": e.seq,
229	            "kind": e.kind,
230	            "summary": e.summary,
231	            # deep-thaw nested FrozenDict/tuple values so the record is plain + JSON-serializable
232	            # (#F-045): dict(e.payload) only shallow-converts and leaves nested FrozenDicts (e.g.
233	            # the baseline 'determinism' map) which json.dumps cannot encode.
234	            "payload": cast("dict[str, Any]", unfreeze(e.payload)),
235	            "timestamp": e.timestamp,
236	            "prev_hash": e.prev_hash,
237	            "entry_hash": e.entry_hash,
238	        }
239	        for e in entries
240	    )
241	
242	
243	def verify_records(
244	    entries: "list[ProvenanceEntry] | tuple[ProvenanceEntry, ...]",
245	    *,
246	    secret: bytes | None = None,
247	) -> None:
248	    """Verify a sequence of entries forms an intact chain (order, linkage, content).
249	
250	    Pass the same ``secret`` the trail was keyed with; an unkeyed trail verifies with
251	    ``secret=None``. A keyed trail will not verify under the wrong/absent secret.
252	    """
253	    prev = _GENESIS
254	    for i, e in enumerate(entries):
255	        if e.seq != i:
256	            raise ProvenanceError(f"provenance entry {i} has seq {e.seq} (reordered or removed)")
257	        if e.prev_hash != prev:
258	            raise ProvenanceError(f"provenance entry {i} prev_hash breaks the chain")
259	        recomputed = _digest(e.seq, e.kind, e.summary, e.payload, e.timestamp, e.prev_hash, secret)
260	        if not hmac.compare_digest(recomputed, e.entry_hash):
261	            raise ProvenanceError(
262	                f"provenance entry {i} content was altered, or the wrong key was used (hash mismatch)"
263	            )
264	        prev = e.entry_hash
265	
266	
267	def from_records(
268	    records: "list[Mapping[str, Any]] | tuple[Mapping[str, Any], ...]",
269	    *,
270	    secret: bytes | None = None,
271	) -> tuple[ProvenanceEntry, ...]:
272	    """Rebuild entries from :meth:`ProvenanceTrail.to_records` output and verify the chain."""
273	    _required = ("seq", "kind", "summary", "payload", "timestamp", "prev_hash", "entry_hash")
274	    rebuilt: list[ProvenanceEntry] = []
275	    for i, r in enumerate(records):
276	        missing = [k for k in _required if k not in r]
277	        if missing:
278	            # A malformed/incompatible trail record must surface as the module's typed error, not a
279	            # raw KeyError, so a caller catching ProvenanceError to handle a corrupt trail file
280	            # covers this case too (pass 4, #G-012).
281	            raise ProvenanceError(f"provenance record {i} is missing field(s) {missing}")
282	        rebuilt.append(
283	            ProvenanceEntry(
284	                seq=r["seq"],
285	                kind=r["kind"],
286	                summary=r["summary"],
287	                payload=r["payload"],
288	                timestamp=r["timestamp"],
289	                prev_hash=r["prev_hash"],
290	                entry_hash=r["entry_hash"],
291	            )
292	        )
293	    entries = tuple(rebuilt)
294	    verify_records(entries, secret=secret)
295	    return entries
296

content.text: Now let me examine the `_canonical` and `_frozen` modules to understand how sets are handled.

content.content:
1	"""Type-faithful canonical hashing — the engine's single content-digest primitive.
2	
3	Two unrelated places need a *stable, type-faithful* content hash: the baseline determinism
4	harness (so the same inputs reproduce the same baseline) and pre-registration (so a lock
5	binds the exact hypothesis content). Both must distinguish values that merely share a
6	``str()`` — ``int 1`` from ``str "1"``, ``date(2020,1,1)`` from ``"2020-01-01"`` — or the
7	hash is a liability rather than a guarantee.
8	
9	This module is the one implementation, hoisted here so it sits *below* both
10	``methodology`` and ``baseline`` in the import graph (neither may depend on the other). It is
11	dependency-free and pure. Anything it cannot canonicalize reproducibly is refused **loudly**:
12	a default (address-based) ``repr`` would make the digest differ every process and silently
13	break the guarantee it exists to provide.
14	"""
15	
16	from __future__ import annotations
17	
18	import datetime as _dt
19	import decimal
20	import hashlib
21	import json
22	import uuid
23	from typing import Any, Mapping
24	
25	
26	def keytag(key: Any) -> str:
27	    """A type-faithful, sortable string for a mapping key, so distinct keys that happen to
28	    share a ``str()`` (e.g. int ``1`` vs str ``"1"``) do NOT collide."""
29	    return f"{type(key).__name__}::{key!r}"
30	
31	
32	def canonical_plain(value: Any) -> Any:
33	    """Canonicalize ``value`` into a fully JSON-native, type-faithful structure for hashing.
34	
35	    Mappings become a sorted list of ``[typed-key, value]`` pairs; bytes, sets, and any
36	    non-JSON-native leaf are tagged with their type rather than stringified. Nothing is coerced
37	    with ``str()``, so the content hash distinguishes values that share a ``str()``.
38	    """
39	    if value is None or isinstance(value, (str, bool, int, float)):
40	        return value  # JSON-native scalars; json encodes 1, "1", true, 1.0 distinctly
41	    if isinstance(value, bytes):
42	        return {"__bytes__": value.hex()}
43	    if isinstance(value, Mapping):
44	        pairs = sorted(
45	            ([keytag(k), canonical_plain(v)] for k, v in value.items()), key=lambda p: p[0]
46	        )
47	        return {"__map__": pairs}
48	    if isinstance(value, (list, tuple)):
49	        return [canonical_plain(v) for v in value]
50	    if isinstance(value, (set, frozenset)):
51	        return {"__set__": sorted((canonical_plain(v) for v in value), key=repr)}
52	    # A small allowlist of leaf types with a value-faithful canonical form.
53	    if isinstance(value, (_dt.date, _dt.datetime, _dt.time)):
54	        return {"__temporal__": value.isoformat()}
55	    if isinstance(value, decimal.Decimal):
56	        return {"__decimal__": str(value)}
57	    if isinstance(value, uuid.UUID):
58	        return {"__uuid__": str(value)}
59	    # Anything else is refused LOUDLY rather than repr-tagged: a default (address-based) repr
60	    # would make the digest differ every process, silently breaking the guarantee.
61	    raise TypeError(
62	        f"cannot canonicalize a leaf of type "
63	        f"{type(value).__module__}.{type(value).__qualname__!r} reproducibly — convert it to a "
64	        "JSON-native value, bytes, date/datetime/Decimal/UUID before hashing"
65	    )
66	
67	
68	def canonical_json(value: Any) -> str:
69	    """The canonical JSON text of ``value`` — stable across processes and key orderings."""
70	    return json.dumps(canonical_plain(value), sort_keys=True, allow_nan=False)
71	
72	
73	def hash_bytes(data: bytes) -> str:
74	    return hashlib.sha256(data).hexdigest()
75	
76	
77	def hash_text(text: str) -> str:
78	    return hash_bytes(text.encode("utf-8"))
79	
80	
81	def hash_value(value: Any) -> str:
82	    """Stable, type-faithful content hash of an arbitrary value.
83	
84	    ``canonical_plain`` canonicalizes everything to JSON-native form first, so ``json.dumps``
85	    needs no ``default=`` coercion — anything it still cannot encode is a real bug to surface,
86	    not to silently stringify.
87	    """
88	    return hash_text(canonical_json(value))
89

1	"""Deep-immutable, hashable mapping for the engine's frozen value records.
2	
3	Many engine dataclasses are ``frozen=True`` and carry mapping fields (``evidence``,
4	``metadata``, ``attributes``, ``params``, ``assertion``, ``provenance``, ``contents``,
5	``determinism``). With a plain ``dict`` default two problems arise (audit pass 1, issue #7):
6	
7	1. ``frozen=True`` auto-generates ``__hash__`` over all fields, so the instances *look*
8	   hashable but raise ``TypeError`` the moment they are hashed (a dict is unhashable).
9	2. ``frozen`` only blocks attribute rebinding; the contained dict stays mutable in place,
10	   so a "frozen" provenance record can be silently altered after construction.
11	
12	:class:`FrozenDict` fixes both: it is an immutable :class:`~collections.abc.Mapping` and is
13	hashable. :func:`freeze` recursively converts nested mappings/sequences/sets so the whole
14	structure is immutable and hashable. Each affected dataclass normalizes its mapping fields
15	through :func:`freeze` in ``__post_init__`` (via ``object.__setattr__`` to satisfy frozen).
16	"""
17	
18	from __future__ import annotations
19	
20	from collections.abc import Mapping
21	from typing import Any, Iterator
22	
23	
24	class FrozenDict(Mapping[str, Any]):
25	    """An immutable, hashable mapping. Item assignment is impossible; hashing is stable."""
26	
27	    __slots__ = ("_data", "_hash")
28	
29	    def __init__(self, data: Mapping[str, Any] | None = None) -> None:
30	        # Freeze values so a directly-constructed FrozenDict satisfies its own
31	        # immutable+hashable contract even from nested-mutable input (audit pass 3, issue #27).
32	        # freeze() is idempotent, so routing through freeze_mapping costs only one extra pass.
33	        self._data: dict[str, Any] = (
34	            {k: freeze(v) for k, v in data.items()} if data is not None else {}
35	        )
36	        self._hash: int | None = None
37	
38	    def __getitem__(self, key: str) -> Any:
39	        return self._data[key]
40	
41	    def __iter__(self) -> Iterator[str]:
42	        return iter(self._data)
43	
44	    def __len__(self) -> int:
45	        return len(self._data)
46	
47	    def __repr__(self) -> str:
48	        return f"FrozenDict({self._data!r})"
49	
50	    def __hash__(self) -> int:
51	        if self._hash is None:
52	            self._hash = hash(frozenset(self._data.items()))
53	        return self._hash
54	
55	    def __eq__(self, other: object) -> bool:
56	        if isinstance(other, Mapping):
57	            return dict(self._data) == dict(other)
58	        return NotImplemented
59	
60	
61	def duck_mapping_items(value: Any) -> list[tuple[Any, Any]] | None:
62	    """Return ``(key, value)`` items if ``value`` is a dict-like NOT registered as a Mapping.
63	
64	    A lazy/proxy dict-like (exposes ``keys()`` + ``__getitem__`` but is not a
65	    :class:`collections.abc.Mapping`) would otherwise be treated as an opaque, hashable-by-
66	    identity leaf by both :func:`freeze` and the measurement↔interpretation fence — passing
67	    through as a live mutable object and escaping the fence scan (#140). Returns ``None`` for
68	    anything that is not such a duck-typed mapping (incl. real Mappings, str/bytes, sequences).
69	    """
70	    if isinstance(value, (str, bytes, bytearray, Mapping)):
71	        return None
72	    keys = getattr(value, "keys", None)
73	    getitem = getattr(value, "__getitem__", None)
74	    if callable(keys) and callable(getitem):
75	        try:
76	            return [(k, value[k]) for k in keys()]
77	        except Exception:  # noqa: BLE001 — a misbehaving dict-like is not a usable mapping
78	            return None
79	    return None
80	
81	
82	def freeze(value: Any) -> Any:
83	    """Recursively convert ``value`` into an immutable, hashable form.
84	
85	    Mappings → :class:`FrozenDict`, lists/tuples → tuples, sets → frozensets. Known mutable
86	    buffers are converted to hashable equivalents (``bytearray`` → ``bytes``;
87	    ``array.array`` / numpy-like arrays exposing ``tobytes`` → a ``(kind, shape, bytes)``
88	    tuple). Any remaining leaf that is not hashable raises ``TypeError`` *here, at
89	    construction time* — rather than silently passing through and resurfacing the error the
90	    moment the record is hashed (audit pass 2, issue #19; the incomplete half of #7).
91	    """
92	    if isinstance(value, FrozenDict):
93	        return value
94	    if isinstance(value, Mapping):
95	        return FrozenDict({k: freeze(v) for k, v in value.items()})
96	    duck = duck_mapping_items(value)
97	    if duck is not None:  # lazy/proxy dict-like — freeze it like a mapping, not a leaf (#140)
98	        return FrozenDict({k: freeze(v) for k, v in duck})
99	    if isinstance(value, (list, tuple)):
100	        return tuple(freeze(v) for v in value)
101	    if isinstance(value, (set, frozenset)):
102	        return frozenset(freeze(v) for v in value)
103	    if isinstance(value, bytearray):
104	        return bytes(value)
105	    # Duck-typed buffer/array support (e.g. array.array, numpy.ndarray) without importing
106	    # numpy: an array-like exposing tobytes() is canonicalized to a hashable descriptor tuple.
107	    # Require an array-ish marker (shape/typecode/dtype) so an unrelated object that merely
108	    # happens to have a tobytes() is left to the hashability check below (fix-review nit, #19).
109	    tobytes = getattr(value, "tobytes", None)
110	    array_like = hasattr(value, "shape") or hasattr(value, "typecode") or hasattr(value, "dtype")
111	    if callable(tobytes) and array_like and not isinstance(value, (bytes, str)):
112	        shape = getattr(value, "shape", None)
113	        kind = getattr(getattr(value, "dtype", None), "str", None) or getattr(
114	            value, "typecode", type(value).__name__
115	        )
116	        # Route the descriptor through freeze() so a malformed tobytes() returning a
117	        # non-hashable value is still caught loud at construction (audit pass 3, issue #26).
118	        return freeze((str(kind), tuple(shape) if shape is not None else None, tobytes()))
119	    try:
120	        hash(value)
121	    except TypeError as exc:
122	        raise TypeError(
123	            f"freeze: leaf of type {type(value).__name__!r} is not hashable and has no "
124	            "known immutable conversion; a frozen value record cannot contain it"
125	        ) from exc
126	    return value
127	
128	
129	def freeze_mapping(value: Mapping[str, Any]) -> FrozenDict:
130	    """Normalize a mapping field to a deep-immutable :class:`FrozenDict`.
131	
132	    Idempotent (pass 3, #F-034): an already-:class:`FrozenDict` value is returned unchanged so
133	    its lazily-cached hash survives. Re-wrapping (e.g. when ``ProvenanceEntry.__post_init__``
134	    freezes a payload that already holds FrozenDict values) would otherwise allocate a fresh
135	    FrozenDict with ``_hash=None`` and force an O(N·depth) re-hash on next use.
136	    """
137	    if isinstance(value, FrozenDict):
138	        return value
139	    return FrozenDict({k: freeze(v) for k, v in value.items()})
140	
141	
142	def unfreeze(value: Any) -> Any:
143	    """Recursively convert a frozen structure back into plain JSON-serializable containers.
144	
145	    The inverse of :func:`freeze` for serialization: :class:`FrozenDict` → ``dict``,
146	    ``frozenset`` → sorted ``list`` (stable order for reproducible output), ``tuple`` →
147	    ``list``. Leaves are returned unchanged. ``json.dumps`` does not know :class:`FrozenDict`
148	    (it is a Mapping, not a ``dict`` subclass), so an operator serializing a deep-frozen public
149	    payload — e.g. ``GateReview.payload`` — must thaw it first (#141).
150	    """
151	    if isinstance(value, (FrozenDict, Mapping)):
152	        return {k: unfreeze(v) for k, v in value.items()}
153	    if isinstance(value, frozenset):
154	        # sort for reproducible output; when elements are not mutually orderable (mixed types),
155	        # fall back to a STABLE key — (type name, repr) — rather than raw set iteration order, which
156	        # is hash-seed/insertion dependent and would make serialized output non-reproducible across
157	        # processes (pass 4, #G-004).
158	        try:
159	            return [unfreeze(v) for v in sorted(value)]
160	        except TypeError:
161	            return [unfreeze(v) for v in sorted(value, key=lambda x: (type(x).__name__, repr(x)))]
162	    if isinstance(value, (list, tuple)):
163	        return [unfreeze(v) for v in value]
164	    return value
165

content.text:
Now I understand the key insight. Let me trace through the roundtrip carefully.

The crucial point: `_digest` (used by `verify_records`) does NOT serialize the payload to JSON directly. It calls `canonical_json(... dict(payload) ...)`, which uses `canonical_plain`. And `canonical_plain` handles sets via `{"__set__": sorted(...)}` — it's type-faithful but the digest is computed over `canonical_plain`, not the raw payload.

The key question is whether a `frozenset` payload value digests the same as a `list` (the form it takes after roundtrip via `unfreeze` → JSON → `from_records`).

Let me reproduce this.

input.command:
cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && source .venv/bin/activate && python -c "
import json
from assay_engine.provenance import ProvenanceTrail, entries_to_records, from_records, verify_records

t = ProvenanceTrail()
t.record('test', 'a set payload', tags={'b', 'a', 'c'})
t.record('test2', 'frozenset payload', fs=frozenset([3,1,2]))

# Verify in-memory trail
t.verify()
print('in-memory verify: OK')

recs = entries_to_records(t.entries)
print('records:', json.dumps(recs, indent=2))

# Roundtrip through JSON (simulating persistent store)
js = json.dumps(recs)
recs2 = json.loads(js)

try:
    rebuilt = from_records(recs2)
    print('from_records (after JSON roundtrip): OK -- VERIFIED')
except Exception as e:
    print('from_records FAILED:', type(e).__name__, e)
"

content.text: The finding reproduces. The roundtrip fails. Let me confirm the exact mechanism — the original digest sees a `frozenset` (canonicalized as `{"__set__": [...]}`), but after roundtrip the payload value is a plain `list`, canonicalized as `[...]`. Different digests.

input.command:
cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && source .venv/bin/activate && python -c "
from assay_engine._canonical import canonical_json
print('frozenset:', canonical_json({'tags': frozenset(['a','b','c'])}))
print('list     :', canonical_json({'tags': ['a','b','c']}))
"

content.text:
Confirmed. The two canonical forms differ (`{"__set__": [...]}` vs `[...]`), so the recomputed hash on the rebuilt chain never matches.

Now let me verify this is a realistic scenario and not out-of-scope. The question is whether any legitimately-recorded payload actually contains a set/frozenset. `freeze` explicitly accepts sets (line 101-102), and `canonical_plain` explicitly handles them (line 50-51), so sets are a supported, intended payload type. Let me check whether real recording paths emit sets.

content.text:
The grep doesn't show explicit set literals in record calls, but the design explicitly supports sets. Let me check whether the recorder path (`as_recorder`/`evidence`) or any gate evidence could carry sets, and verify the in-memory `verify()` works but the persisted roundtrip is the failure.

The core defect is proven regardless: `freeze` and `canonical_plain` both explicitly and deliberately accept set/frozenset as valid payload values, and `entries_to_records` deliberately thaws them to lists via `unfreeze`. The roundtrip is asymmetric — `freeze` preserves set-ness for the digest, but `unfreeze` converts to list, and `from_records` re-freezes the list as a tuple (not a set). So a legitimately-recorded set payload cannot be persisted and re-verified.

Let me confirm the re-freeze produces a tuple not a set on rebuild.

input.command:
cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && source .venv/bin/activate && python -c "
from assay_engine._frozen import freeze
print('orig frozenset frozen:', repr(freeze(frozenset([1,2,3]))))
print('roundtripped list frozen:', repr(freeze([1,2,3])))
"

content.content:
orig frozenset frozen: frozenset({1, 2, 3})
roundtripped list frozen: (1, 2, 3)

content.text:
Fully confirmed. The asymmetry is structural:

- Original payload: `frozenset({1,2,3})` → digested as `{"__set__": [...]}`
- After `to_records` (`unfreeze`): becomes plain `list`
- After JSON + `from_records`: re-frozen as `tuple` → digested as `[...]`

The digest over the rebuilt entry differs from the stored `entry_hash`, so `verify_records` raises a hash-mismatch — falsely reporting tamper/corruption on an untampered, legitimately-recorded trail.

This is a genuine defect:
- Sets/frozensets are an explicitly supported payload type (`freeze` line 101-102, `canonical_plain` line 50-51) — not out-of-scope opaque input.
- The persist→reload→verify path is the documented purpose of `to_records`/`from_records` ("a caller holding only the returned entries... can still persist a re-checkable trail").
- It breaks the tamper-evidence guarantee in the false-alarm direction: a clean trail fails verification, undermining the integrity claim and reproducibility of any study whose provenance happens to record a set.

It reproduces deterministically with no special harness configuration.

input.reasoning:
Reproduced deterministically. Set/frozenset payload values do not survive the to_records→(JSON)→from_records→verify_records roundtrip; verification falsely raises a hash mismatch on a clean, untampered trail.

Root cause (asymmetric freeze/unfreeze vs. type-faithful digest):
- The entry digest is computed by _digest → canonical_json → canonical_plain, which is TYPE-FAITHFUL: a frozenset canonicalizes to {"__set__": [...]} (src/assay_engine/_canonical.py:50-51), distinct from a list's [...] .
- A payload set/frozenset is a fully supported value: freeze() explicitly converts set/frozenset→frozenset (src/assay_engine/_frozen.py:101-102) and ProvenanceEntry.__post_init__ freezes the payload (provenance.py:66). So the ORIGINAL entry_hash is computed over a frozenset → {"__set__": ...}.
- entries_to_records() deep-thaws via unfreeze(), which turns frozenset → sorted list (_frozen.py:153-161), so the persisted record carries a plain list (verified: payload {"tags": ["a","b","c"]}).
- from_records() rebuilds the ProvenanceEntry from that list; freeze() then converts list → tuple (not set) (_frozen.py:99-100, confirmed: freeze([1,2,3]) == (1,2,3)). canonical_plain serializes the tuple as a bare [...] array, NOT {"__set__": ...}.
- Result: recomputed digest != stored entry_hash → verify_records raises ProvenanceError "content was altered ... (hash mismatch)" at entry 0.

Reproduction (exact): recorded tags={'a','b','c'} and fs=frozenset([1,2,3]); in-memory t.verify() passes, but from_records(json roundtrip) raises ProvenanceError on entry 0. Confirmed canonical_json divergence: frozenset→{"__set__":[...]} vs list→[...].

Scope/severity: Not out-of-scope. Sets are a deliberately supported payload type (both freeze and canonical_plain have dedicated set branches), and persist→reload→verify is the documented purpose of to_records/from_records ("a caller holding only the returned entries can still persist a re-checkable trail"). The bug breaks tamper-evidence in the false-positive direction: a legitimately-recorded, untampered trail fails verification, which both blocks reproducibility and erodes trust in the integrity signal. The in-memory trail verifies, masking the defect until a study's provenance is round-tripped through a store. High severity is justified.

content.text:
Verified P10-MO-1 as a TRUE_POSITIVE. The set/frozenset roundtrip defect reproduces deterministically: an in-memory trail with a set/frozenset payload verifies fine, but after `to_records`→JSON→`from_records` the chain fails verification with a false hash-mismatch (`ProvenanceError` on entry 0).

Root cause is an asymmetry between the type-faithful digest and the freeze/unfreeze path, all in `/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/`:
- `_canonical.py:50-51` — `canonical_plain` digests a frozenset as `{"__set__": [...]}`, distinct from a list's `[...]`.
- `_frozen.py:153-161` — `unfreeze` (used by `entries_to_records`) thaws frozenset → sorted **list**.
- `_frozen.py:99-100` — on rebuild, `from_records` re-freezes that list → **tuple**, which canonicalizes as a bare array, never `{"__set__": ...}`.

So the recomputed digest can never match the stored `entry_hash`. Sets are an explicitly supported payload type and persist→reload→verify is the documented purpose of these functions, so this is in scope and breaks the tamper-evidence guarantee in the false-alarm direction.
